title: Ode On The Poetical Character
content:
As once, if not with light regard, 
I read aright that gifted bard,
(Him whose school above the rest
His loveliest Elfin Queen has blest,)
One, only one, unrivalï¿½d fair,
Might hope the magic girdle wear,
At solemn tourney hung on high,
The wish of each love-darting eye;
Lo! to each other nymph in turn applied,
As if, in air unseen, some hovï¿½ring hand,
Some chaste and angel-friend to virgin-fame,
With whisperï¿½d spell had burst the starting band,
It left unblest her loathï¿½d dishonourï¿½d side;
Happier, hopeless fair, if never
Her baffled hand with vain endeavour
Had touchï¿½d that fatal zone to her denied!
Young Fancy thus, to me divinest name,
To whom, preparï¿½d and bathï¿½d in Heavï¿½n,
The cest of amplest powï¿½r is givï¿½n:
To few the god-like gift assigns,
To gird their blest prophetic loins,
And gaze her visions wild, and feel unmixï¿½d her flame!

The band, as fairy legends say,
Was wove on that creating day,
When He, who callï¿½d with thought to birth
Yon tented sky, this laughing earth,
And dressï¿½d with springs, and forests tall,
And pourï¿½d the main engirting all,
Long by the lovï¿½d enthusiast wooï¿½d,
Himself in some diviner mood,
Retiring, sate with her alone,
And placï¿½d her on his sapphire throne,
The whiles, the vaulted shrine around,
Seraphic wires were heard to sound,
Now sublimest triumph swelling,
Now on love and mercy dwelling;
And she, from out the veiling cloud,
Breathï¿½d her magic notes aloud:
And thou, thou rich-hairï¿½d youth of morn,
And all thy subject life was born!
The dangï¿½rous Passions kept aloof,
Far from the sainted growing woof:
But near it sate ecstatic Wonder
Listï¿½ning the deep applauding thunder:
And Truth, in sunny vest arrayï¿½d,
By whose the tarselï¿½s eyes were made
All the shadï¿½wy tribes of mind,
In braided dance their murmurs joinï¿½d,
And all the bright uncounted Powï¿½rs
Who feed on Heavï¿½nï¿½s ambrosial flowï¿½rs.
Where is the bard, whose soul can now
Its high presuming hopes avow?
Where he who thinks, with rapture blind,
This hallowï¿½d work for him designï¿½d?

High on some cliff, to Heavï¿½n up-pilï¿½d,
Of rude access, of prospect wild,
Where, tangled round the jealous steep,
Strange shades oï¿½erbrow the valleys deep,
And holy genii guard the rock,
Its glooms embrown, its springs unlock,
While on its rich ambitious head,
An Eden, like his own, lies spread.
I view that oak, the fancied glades among,
By which as Milton lay, his evï¿½ning ear,
From many a cloud that droppï¿½d ethereal dew,
Nigh spherï¿½d in Heavï¿½n its native strains could hear:
On which that ancient trump he reachï¿½d was hung;
Thither oft his glory greeting,
From Wallerï¿½s myrtle shades retreating,
With many a vow from Hopeï¿½s aspiring tongue,
My trembling feet his guiding steps pursue;
In vain, such bliss to one alone,
Of all the sons of soul was known,
And Heavï¿½n, and Fancy, kindred powï¿½rs,
Have now oï¿½erturnï¿½d thï¿½ inspiring bowï¿½rs,
Or curtainï¿½d close such scene from evï¿½ry future view.
meaning:
About: William Collins (25 December 1721 ï¿½ 12 June 1759), English poet

Second in influence only to Thomas Gray, he was an important poet of the middle decades of the 18th century. His lyrical odes mark a turn away from the Augustan poetry of Alexander Pope's generation and towards the romantic era which would soon follow.
